text.title: Tool Definition Quality and Security
text.summary: Rules for writing tool descriptions that maximize correct selection, handling errors structurally, and gating destructive tools before execution.

rule.statement: Write tool descriptions as if explaining to a junior developer who does not know the system — the model uses the description, not the code, to decide when to call a tool.
rule.rationale: Vague descriptions are the #1 cause of wrong tool selection. A description that says "get data" is worse than one that says "fetch current weather conditions for a city by calling the external weather API".

rule.statement: Return structured error objects from tools ({"error": "reason"}) instead of raising exceptions; let the model decide how to recover.
rule.rationale: Exceptions propagate out of the tool loop and crash the agent. A structured error lets the model reason about failure and potentially retry with different arguments.

rule.statement: Sandbox any code execution tool (subprocess, exec) to prevent model-generated code from accessing the host filesystem or network without restriction.
rule.rationale: A model-generated os.system("rm -rf /") in an unsandboxed subprocess is a critical security incident. Code execution tools must be isolated.

rule.statement: Require explicit human confirmation before executing any tool that modifies external state (database writes, API calls that create/delete resources, email sending).
rule.rationale: Autonomous tool execution without approval is appropriate only for read-only tools. Destructive actions triggered by ambiguous natural language intent cause irreversible harm.

antipattern.description: Passing large tool results (>50KB) back to the model without truncation.
antipattern.reason: Very large tool results inflate context dramatically, pushing other messages out of the window and causing the model to lose track of earlier instructions.

antipattern.description: Executing parallel tool calls (OpenAI default) when tools share mutable state.
antipattern.reason: Parallel calls can produce race conditions — e.g., two concurrent writes to the same database row. Use sequential execution for stateful tools.

rule.statement: Execute independent tool calls in parallel using asyncio.gather; log each tool call with name, args, result, and latency to a persistent trace.
rule.rationale: Sequential execution of N independent tools multiplies latency by N. Parallel execution with async implementations reduces multi-tool workflows to the slowest single call. Trace logs enable replay on failure.
